[
  {
    "type": "Bug",
    "description": "函数 `clientTargetForAddress` 在处理 Windows 命名管道路径时，生成的 gRPC 目标字符串格式不正确。对于 Windows 命名管道路径（例如 `//./pipe/containerd-containerd`），函数会返回 `\"passthrough://///./pipe/containerd-containerd\"`。这可能导致 gRPC 客户端无法正确解析该路径，因为 `passthrough:///` 后面跟了四个斜杠（`////`），而正确的格式应该是三个斜杠（`///`）。这可能会在 Windows 平台上导致连接失败。",
    "evidence": "文件: staging/src/k8s.io/cri-client/pkg/connection_target.go，第 27-30 行：`if strings.HasPrefix(addr, \"/\") { return \"passthrough:///\" + addr }`。测试文件 `connection_target_test.go` 中显示对于 Windows 管道路径 `\"//./pipe/containerd-containerd\"`，期望的输出是 `\"passthrough://///./pipe/containerd-containerd\"`，这包含了四个斜杠。",
    "advice": "修改 `clientTargetForAddress` 函数，确保对于以 `/` 开头的路径（包括 Unix socket 和 Windows 命名管道），正确拼接 `\"passthrough:///\"` 前缀。对于 Windows 命名管道路径 `\"//./pipe/containerd-containerd\"`，拼接后应为 `\"passthrough:////./pipe/containerd-containerd\"`（即三个斜杠后跟路径本身）。需要审查 gRPC 的 passthrough resolver 对目标字符串格式的确切要求，并相应调整代码和测试用例。",
    "confidence": 0.8,
    "severity": "Medium"
  },
  {
    "type": "Readability",
    "description": "函数 `clientTargetForAddress` 中的注释 `\"Defensive: normalize callers that pass a unix endpoint instead of the parsed socket path.\"` 可能具有误导性。该函数不仅处理 Unix socket 端点，还处理 Windows 命名管道路径和 TCP 地址。注释应更准确地反映函数的实际行为，即处理各种 CRI 端点地址格式，并为基于文件的路径（Unix socket 和 Windows 命名管道）添加 passthrough resolver 前缀。",
    "evidence": "文件: staging/src/k8s.io/cri-client/pkg/connection_target.go，第 20-22 行：注释 `\"Defensive: normalize callers that pass a unix endpoint instead of the parsed socket path.\"` 仅提到了 Unix endpoint。但函数逻辑和测试用例表明它也处理 Windows 管道路径和 TCP 地址。",
    "advice": "更新函数注释，以更全面地描述其目的：处理 CRI 端点地址字符串，规范化 Unix socket 端点格式，并为基于文件的路径（Unix socket 和 Windows 命名管道）添加 gRPC passthrough resolver 前缀，以确保自定义拨号器能接收到原始路径。",
    "confidence": 0.9,
    "severity": "Low"
  },
  {
    "type": "Design",
    "description": "新增的测试函数 `TestNewRemoteImageServiceUnixSocketEndpoint` 和 `TestNewRemoteRuntimeServiceUnixSocketEndpoint` 在清理阶段直接使用 `os.Remove` 删除 socket 文件，这存在潜在风险。如果 `util.GetAddressAndDialer` 返回的 `addr` 不是文件路径（例如是 TCP 地址），`os.Remove` 可能会失败或产生意外行为。虽然当前测试上下文是 Unix socket，但代码模式不够健壮，且与项目中对资源清理的常见实践可能不一致。",
    "evidence": "文件: staging/src/k8s.io/cri-client/pkg/remote_image_test.go，第 108-117 行；文件: staging/src/k8s.io/cri-client/pkg/remote_runtime_test.go，第 119-128 行。清理代码：`if addr, _, err := util.GetAddressAndDialer(endpoint); err == nil { if _, err := os.Stat(addr); err == nil { if err := os.Remove(addr); err != nil { t.Errorf(\"remove %q: %v\", addr, err) } } }`。",
    "advice": "建议重构清理逻辑，使其更健壮和清晰。可以检查 `addr` 是否确实是一个文件路径（例如，对于 Unix socket，路径以 `/` 开头），然后再尝试删除。或者，考虑在测试的 fake runtime 停止后，由其自身负责清理其创建的 socket 文件，使测试函数更专注于验证客户端连接行为。",
    "confidence": 0.7,
    "severity": "Low"
  }
]

commits: [{'sha': 'be5285b46fbe26ad41988c3800e38353acad18ae', 'message': 'Fix restartable init container startup race'}]
review_comments:
["does this probe happen immediately or always wait PeriodSeconds before the first attempt? if it happens immediately, does it always fail on the first attempt so the first success we expect is always at least a second later? I'm trying to understand if this is effectively adding a minimum 1 second delay into the startup and if that is masking races the test would have caught previously", "(if the point of the test is not to flush out sub-second races in container startup order, maybe injecting minimum one-second delays is fine, I just didn't know if we were relying on this test for fast ordering behavior)", "I'm not sure exactly what this was trying to verify before, but the reality is > Restartable init containers have guaranteed startup ordering, but not guaranteed execution ordering. This is sort of related to the exit code 2 issue we were seeing, in that the actual runc exec is asynchronous. If you want guaranteed execution ordering, you need a secondary check. --- > does this probe happen immediately or always wait PeriodSeconds before the first attempt? The probe management is super weird (and I take full responsibility for it, and have a low-priority item to fix it...). Basically, it starts a go-routine for all the probes as soon as the pod is initialized in the Kubelet, and the probes watch for containers to be created / recreated and react accordingly. So to answer your question: neither, it's a non-deterministic interval between 0 and PeriodSeconds :grimacing: That said, I actually think a post-start hook is a better way of handling this. PostStart hooks are executed immediately, and it's a synchronous call. I'll make this change.", 'sounds good... will the hook need to be adjusted to poll until the file is available, since retry doesn't look built into PostStartHook? something more like (completely untested) `["sh", "-c", "while [ ! -f started ]; do echo "waiting for started $(date)" && sleep 1; done"]`', "Done. I used a trick with `tail` which I think uses inotify to avoid polling. I'm not sure how portable this is though... but I'm not sure how to test which utilities are available in the windows container. I'm tempted to just mark this as a linux-only test. WDYT?", 'Or I guess I could just switch to the polling version, which probably (?) works on windows', "doesn't the `-f` keep tail running until the timeout? I thought we wanted this to exit with success as soon as `started` exists", "I... don't really know... whatever we can do to make the hook exit as quickly as possible once `started` exists, and avoid introducing another race, and not introduce new platform restrictions? I don't really know the domain well enough to know what to recommend to accomplish that", '`| read` is a hack to terminate the process, but on closer inspection this is not that portable and not actually any faster than a short-interval poll, so I swapped it back to a simple 100ms poll.', 'make sure we definitely have busybox versions with support for fractional second sleeps, then lgtm (once we demonstrate clean runs of the modified test cases)', 'Yep, I verified it.', '/lgtm /hold kind of cool that we reach that level of speed on status updates.', '/lgtm /approve /retest', 'This issue is currently awaiting triage. ["does this probe happen immediately or always wait PeriodSeconds before the first attempt? if it happens immediately, does it always fail on the first attempt so the first success we expect is always at least a second later? I'm trying to understand if this is effectively adding a minimum 1 second delay into the startup and if that is masking races the test would have caught previously", "(if the point of the test is not to flush out sub-second races in container startup order, maybe injecting minimum one-second delays is fine, I just didn't know if we were relying on this test for fast ordering behavior)", "I'm not sure exactly what this was trying to verify before, but the reality is > Restartable init containers have guaranteed startup ordering, but not guaranteed execution ordering. This is sort of related to the exit code 2 issue we were seeing, in that the actual runc exec is asynchronous. If you want guaranteed execution ordering, you need a secondary check. --- > does this probe happen immediately or always wait PeriodSeconds before the first attempt? The probe management is super weird (and I take full responsibility for it, and have a low-priority item to fix it...). Basically, it starts a go-routine for all the probes as soon as the pod is initialized in the Kubelet, and the probes watch for containers to be created / recreated and react accordingly. So to answer your question: neither, it's a non-deterministic interval between 0 and PeriodSeconds :grimacing: That said, I actually think a post-start hook is a better way of handling this. PostStart hooks are executed immediately, and it's a synchronous call. I'll make this change.", 'sounds good... will the hook need to be adjusted to poll until the file is available, since retry doesn't look built into PostStartHook? something more like (completely untested) `["sh", "-c", "while [ ! -f started ]; do echo "waiting for started $(date)" && sleep 1; done"]`', "Done. I used a trick with `tail` which I think uses inotify to avoid polling. I'm not sure how portable this is though... but I'm not sure how to test which utilities are available in the windows container. I'm tempted to just mark this as a linux-only test. WDYT?", 'Or I guess I could just switch to the polling version, which probably (?) works on windows', "doesn't the `-f` keep tail running until the timeout? I thought we wanted this to exit with success as soon as `started` exists", "I... don't really know... whatever we can do to make the hook exit as quickly as possible once `started` exists, and avoid introducing another race, and not introduce new platform restrictions? I don't really know the domain well enough to know what to recommend to accomplish that", '`| read` is a hack to terminate the process, but on closer inspection this is not that portable and not actually any faster than a short-interval poll, so I swapped it back to a simple 100ms poll.', 'make sure we definitely have busybox versions with support for fractional second sleeps, then lgtm (once we demonstrate clean runs of the modified test cases)', 'Yep, I verified it.', '/lgtm /hold kind of cool that we reach that level of speed on status updates.', '/lgtm /approve /retest', 'This issue is currently awaiting triage. If a SIG or subproject determines this is a relevant issue, they will accept it by applying the `triage/accepted` label and provide further guidance. [{'sha': 'e2660960f2e0eca0fb49c57091d712d604e702f6', 'message': "Add topology-aware routing to winkernel proxier

The winkernel proxier's `OnTopologyChange` was so far a no-op, Windows kube-proxy ignored EndpointSlice topology hints and always load-balanced across all ready endpoints regardless of zone or node proximity.

This change wires the winkernel proxier into the shared `CategorizeEndpoints` in `topology.go` to support topology-aware routing:

- Implement `OnTopologyChange` to store topology labels and force re-evaluation of all services via `cleanupAllPolicies`.

- Introduce zoneHints/nodeHints fields in `endpointInfo` and propagate them from `BaseEndpointInfo` in `newEndpointInfo`.

- Use `CategorizeEndpoints` in `syncProxyRules` to obtain topology-filtered cluster/local/allReachable endpoint lists. Iterate `allReachableEndpoints` (instead of raw `endpointsMap`) and classify HNS endpoints into hnsClusterEndpoints/hnsLocalEndpoints using endpoint key sets built from `CategorizeEndpoints` output.

- Use `hnsClusterEndpoints` as the default for ClusterIP, NodePort,ExternalIP, and LB Ingress load balancers, switching to `hnsLocalEndpoints` based on existing DSR-aware condition (localTrafficDSR, preserveDIP, internalTrafficLocal).

This change preserves existing DSR behavior:
- LB endpoint selection uses `svcInfo.localTrafficDSR` (which is false when DSR is not supported) rather than `ExternalPolicyLocal`.
- `endpointsAvailableForLB` continues to use DSR-aware `isAllEndpointsTerminating`/`isAllEndpointsNonServing` checks.

Backward compatible: when `topologyLabels` is nil (no `OnTopologyChange` received), `CategorizeEndpoints` returns all ready endpoints with no topology filtering, identical to previous behavior."}]

in remoteRuntimeService and remoteImageService to eliminate a data

during Unimplemented fallback.

review:
[
  {
    "type": "Bug",
    "description": "代码变更将 `useStreaming` 字段从 `bool` 类型改为 `atomic.Bool` 类型，以修复数据竞争。然而，在 `remoteImageService` 和 `remoteRuntimeService` 的构造函数中，初始化 `useStreaming` 字段的顺序存在潜在问题。在 `remoteImageService` 的构造函数中，`service.useStreaming.Store(useStreaming)` 是在结构体初始化之后调用的。虽然这本身没有问题，但考虑到 Kubernetes 项目对并发安全的高标准要求，以及 `atomic.Bool` 的零值（false）可能与传入的 `useStreaming` 参数（true）不同，如果结构体在 `Store` 调用之前被泄露或使用，可能会导致短暂的错误行为。虽然风险很低，但为了保持最高标准的健壮性，这是一个值得注意的细节。",
    "evidence": "文件: staging/src/k8s.io/cri-client/pkg/remote_image.go\n+  service.useStreaming.Store(useStreaming)\n文件: staging/src/k8s.io/cri-client/pkg/remote_runtime.go\n+  service.useStreaming.Store(useStreaming)",
    "advice": "建议在结构体初始化时直接设置 `atomic.Bool` 的值，或者确保在结构体完全初始化（包括所有原子字段）之前，不会将其暴露给其他 goroutine。一个更简洁的做法是使用 `atomic.Bool` 的 `CompareAndSwap` 或确保在构造函数返回之前完成所有初始化。然而，鉴于当前代码中 `validateServiceConnection` 在 `Store` 之后调用，且没有泄露 `service`，风险极低。为了保持代码清晰和一致性，可以考虑在结构体字面量中初始化 `atomic.Bool`，但 Go 的 `atomic.Bool` 没有直接的导出字段，因此当前模式是可接受的。审查主要确认修复了数据竞争，这是一个正确的改进。",
    "confidence": 0.6,
    "severity": "Low"
  },
  {
    "type": "Readability",
    "description": "代码变更中，`remoteImageService` 和 `remoteRuntimeService` 结构体的 `useStreaming` 字段注释已更新，以反映从 `bool` 到 `atomic.Bool` 的更改。注释提到“多个 goroutine 可能同时观察到 Unimplemented 并存储 false，但这是无害的，因为存储是幂等的”。虽然这解释了并发行为，但注释没有明确说明 `atomic.Bool` 的使用确保了读/写的原子性，从而修复了数据竞争。对于不熟悉 `atomic.Bool` 的开发者，注释可以更明确地说明其目的。",
    "evidence": "文件: staging/src/k8s.io/cri-client/pkg/remote_image.go\n+// useStreaming indicates whether to use streaming RPCs for list operations\n+// when the CRIListStreaming feature gate is enabled. It falls back to false\n+// if a streaming RPC returns Unimplemented. Multiple goroutines may\n+// concurrently observe Unimplemented and store false, but that is harmless\n+// because the store is idempotent.\n+ useStreaming atomic.Bool",
    "advice": "建议更新注释，明确指出 `atomic.Bool` 用于确保对 `useStreaming` 字段的并发访问是原子性的，从而避免数据竞争。例如，可以添加一句：“使用 atomic.Bool 确保对该字段的读写是原子操作，防止数据竞争。”",
    "confidence": 0.8,
    "severity": "Low"
  }
]

[{'sha': 'f1e81f8a24a4e7deadd1430f96a55e65e61f04d3', 'message': "kubelet/dra: roll back prepared drivers on partial NodePrepareResources failure

When a Pod has ResourceClaims served by more than one DRA driver,
NodePrepareResources is called once per driver.  If driver A succeeds
but driver B subsequently fails, the existing code returns an error
immediately without calling NodeUnprepareResources on driver A.  This
leaves driver A's devices in a prepared/reserved state indefinitely,
effectively stranding capacity until the driver's own reconciliation
eventually reclaims it.

Fix: introduce a preparedByDriver list that records each driver that
has successfully returned from NodePrepareResources in the current
prepare call.  Any error path in the batch loop now calls
rollbackPrepared(), which issues NodeUnprepareResources for every
driver that already completed.  Rollback errors are logged but not
propagated so that the original prepare error is not shadowed.

The same rollback is also triggered when:
  - A driver's response references an unknown claim UID.
  - A per-claim result carries an error string.
  - A driver skips some claims (unfinished != 0).
  - Writing CDI device data to the local cache fails.

Fixes #137819"}, {'sha': '0bbdc10bbb1309dd368623ef1ce5fc1f8ed6f4b7', 'message': 'kubelet/dra: add regression test for partial prepare rollback

Adds TestPrepareResourcesRollbackOnPartialFailure to
pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/manager_test.go.  The test exercises the scenario
from #137819 where a Pod requires two ResourceClaims managed by two
different DRA drivers, driver A succeeds, and driver B then returns a
per-claim error.  It asserts that:

  - PrepareResources returns an error referencing the failing claim.
  - Driver A receives exactly one NodeUnprepareResources call (rollback).
  - Driver B receives no NodeUnprepareResources call.
  - Neither claim is marked as prepared in the cache.'}, {'sha': '946d424bc8aef1779733dd1698364f115f256be5', 'message': 'kubelet/dra: add regression test for partial prepare rollback

Adds TestPrepareResourcesRollbackOnPartialFailure to
pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/manager_test.go.  The test exercises the scenario
from #137819 where a Pod requires two ResourceClaims managed by two
different DRA drivers, driver A succeeds, and driver B then returns a
per-claim error.  It asserts that:

  - PrepareResources returns an error referencing the failing claim.
  - Driver A receives exactly one NodeUnprepareResources call (rollback).
  - Driver B receives no NodeUnprepareResources call.
  - Neither claim is marked as prepared in the cache.'}]

', '> This leaves driver A's devices in a prepared/reserved state indefinitely, stranding capacity until the driver's own reconciliation reclaims it and leaving the Pod stuck in FailedPrepareDynamicResources. This doesn't make sense to me. Even if driver A's NodeUnprepareResources gets called, the pod will still be in a pending state and the device continue to be reserved for it. What is "driver's own reconciliation"? How did you run into this problem? Can you explain a bit more the real-world implications for you and how this PR helps?', 'This is about actual device stranding, not scheduler state. working on reported issue #137819 were users experienced device leaks in multi-driver setups. The problem: when a pod needs GPU + network card, GPU driver succeeds but network driver fails, the GPU stays "prepared" forever-unusable by other pods. In prod ML. clusters, this means $10K+ GPUs sit idle while workloads queue up waiting for "available" resources that are actually stranded. Users see their training jobs stuck waiting for hardware that's technically free but locked in prepared state. Now, drivers do have their own reconciliation loops that periodically scan for orphaned prepared resources and clean them up, but these run every 30+ sec. So, you get these expensive gaps where hardware sits idle even though workloads are desperately waiting for it - multiply that across dozens of jobs and it creates real delays and wasted compute spend. fix calls NodeUnprepareResources() immediately when other drivers fail, so devices get released right away instead of waiting for the next cleanup cycle. Test PrepareResources RollbackOnPartialFailure validates this scenario works correctly!', '@Retr0-XD: can you put your response in a rhyme? I have problems understanding what you mean with "drivers do have their own reconciliation loops". ', 'Hey @Retr0-XD, thanks for the PR! Two things came into my mind when reviewing the PR - Resource preparation failure for a driver may be transient. Instead of invoking`NodeUnprepareResources` immediately after a driver fails to prepare the device from a driver, it might be worth considering retrying with backoff, and only unprepare all devices after a specific timeout is reached. This will allow Kubelet to reissue `NodePrepareResources` calls with the hope that subsequent retries may succeed. - Even if Kubelet calls NodeUnprepareResources, it still does not free up the ResourceClaim in the API/scheduling sense. Currently, the ResourceClaim controller clears the ResourceClaim `.status.allocation` when `.status.reservedFor` is empty, which happens when the pod is deleted.', '/close https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/137819#issuecomment-4108393342 ', '@pohly: Closed this PR.

If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the [kubernetes-sigs/prow](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/prow/issues/new?title=Prow%20issue:) repository.
']

In prod ML. clusters, this means $10K+ GPUs sit idle while workloads queue up waiting for "available" resources that are actually stranded. Users see their training jobs stuck waiting for hardware that's technically free but locked in prepared state. Now, drivers do have their own reconciliation loops that periodically scan for orphaned prepared resources and clean them up, but these run every 30+ sec. So, you get these expensive gaps where hardware sits idle even though workloads are desperately waiting for it - multiply that across dozens of jobs and it creates real delays and wasted compute spend. fix calls NodeUnprepareResources() immediately when other drivers fail, so devices get released right away instead of waiting for the next cleanup cycle.     "description": "在 `run()` 方法中，`r.FlushMetrics()` 被移到了 `select` 语句之前。这意味着在每次循环迭代开始时，无论 `stopCh` 是否已关闭，都会立即执行一次刷新。这与原始逻辑不符，原始逻辑是在每次循环迭代的末尾（`time.Sleep` 之后）才刷新。这可能导致在收到停止信号后，仍然执行了一次额外的、不必要的刷新操作。虽然这可能不会导致功能错误，但改变了原有的行为，可能影响测试的预期。",

 	for {
+		r.FlushMetrics()
 		select {
 		case <-r.stopCh:

 			return
-		default:

 		}
-		r.FlushMetrics()

 }
+
+func TestNewMetricsAsyncRecorder(t *testing.T) {
+	synctest.Test(t, func(t *testing.T) {
+		Register()
+
+		bufferSize := 10
+		interval := 1 * time.Second
+		stop := make(chan struct{})
+
+		r := NewMetricsAsyncRecorder(bufferSize, interval, stop)
+
+		var buffered int
+		var flushed uint64
+		check := func(step string) {
+			t.Helper()
+
+			actualBuffered := len(r.bufferCh)
+			if actualBuffered != buffered {
+				t.Errorf("%s: expected %d elements in the buffer waiting to be flushed, got %d", step, buffered, actualBuffered)
+			}
+
+			actualFlushed, err := testutil.GetHistogramMetricCount(PluginExecutionDuration.WithLabelValues("", "", ""))
+			if err != nil {
+				t.Errorf("%s: error getting metric: %v", step, err)
+			}
+			if actualFlushed != flushed {
+				t.Errorf("%s: expected metric to have count %v, got %v", step, flushed, actualFlushed)
+			}
+		}
+
+		check("nothing should be buffered or flushed immediately after starting")
+
+		synctest.Wait()
+		r.ObservePluginDurationAsync("", "", "", 1)
+		buffered++
+		check("a metric observed immediately after flushing should only be buffered")
+
+		time.Sleep(interval / 2)
+		r.ObservePluginDurationAsync("", "", "", 1)
+		buffered++
+		check("a metric observed during the interval should only be buffered")
+
+		time.Sleep(interval / 2)
+		synctest.Wait()
+		flushed += uint64(buffered)
+		buffered = 0
+		check("when the interval is reached, buffered metrics should be flushed")
+
+		r.ObservePluginDurationAsync("", "", "", 1)
+		time.Sleep(interval)
+		synctest.Wait()
+		flushed += 1
+		check("metrics should be flushed at each interval")
+
+		r.ObservePluginDurationAsync("", "", "", 1)
+		time.Sleep(interval / 2)
+		buffered++
+		check("metrics should continue to be buffered during the last interval")
+
+		closedAt := time.Now()
+		close(stop)
+		<-r.IsStoppedCh
+		if actualStopDelay := time.Since(closedAt); actualStopDelay != 0 {
+			t.Errorf("recorder was stopped halfway through its %v interval, expected it to stop immediately but it actually stopped after %v", interval, actualStopDelay)
+		}
+
+		check("buffered metrics are not flushed after stopping")
+	})

[{'sha': '69b6a33901fb75c1ff1a23cdbb16b7f459fc38d4', 'message': 'scheduler metrics: Add async recorder test'}, {'sha': 'a408aac9897c0494d4328a069e730a0ec2caa12a', 'message': "scheduler metrics: Let async recorder stop immediately

A metrics.MetricAsyncRecorder's background goroutine calls time.Sleep to
control the frequency at which buffered metrics are flushed. time.Sleep
cannot be interrupted, so when the recorder is signalled to stop by
closing its stopCh, it must wait until it finishes sleeping before
noticing that the stopCh is closed. Blocking in `select` on either
stopCh or a channel returned by time.After allows the wait to be
interrupted and the recorder to stop immediately."}]
